title: Stacks & Queues
description: Stacks and queues are fundamental to dozens of interview problems. JavaScript arrays serve as both — master push/pop for stacks and shift/push for queues, plus classic patterns like valid parentheses and BFS.

sections.title: Array as a stack (LIFO)
sections.explanation: Use push() and pop() to use an array as a stack — both are O(1). Stacks appear in expression evaluation, undo operations, DFS, and the classic valid parentheses problem. Check `stack.length === 0` instead of comparing to null.

exercise.prompt: Push values 10, 20, 30 onto a stack. Pop twice, printing each popped value. Then print the remaining stack length.

validation.message: Pop gives 30 then 20 (LIFO order), leaving length 1
Use push to add and pop to remove from the stack

exercise.hint: Push 30, then pop() returns 30 first (last in, first out), then 20.

sections.title: Array as a queue (FIFO)
sections.explanation: Use push() to enqueue and shift() to dequeue for a basic FIFO queue. Note: shift() is O(n) because it reindexes all elements. For interview purposes this is acceptable, but mention the trade-off if asked. For true O(1), you'd implement a linked list queue.

exercise.prompt: Enqueue values 10, 20, 30 using push. Dequeue twice with shift, printing each value. Print the remaining length.

validation.message: Shift gives 10 then 20 (FIFO order), leaving length 1
Use push to enqueue and shift to dequeue

exercise.hint: shift() removes from the front — gives 10 first (first in, first out).

sections.title: Valid parentheses pattern
sections.explanation: The classic stack problem: for each opening bracket, push it. For each closing bracket, pop and check it matches. If the stack is empty at the end and all brackets matched, the string is valid. This pattern extends to any nested-structure validation.

exercise.prompt: Implement isValid for parentheses strings containing only '(' and ')'. Push on '(', pop on ')'. Return false if pop fails or stack isn't empty. Test with "(())", "()", and "(()", printing each result.

validation.message: "(())" and "()" are valid, "((" is not — unclosed parenthesis
Use push/pop for stack operations and length check for validation

exercise.hint: push on '(', pop on ')'. Check stack.length === 0 at the end for validity.

sections.title: BFS with a queue
sections.explanation: BFS uses a queue to process nodes level by level. Push neighbors into the queue, then shift to process the next node. Combine with a visited Set to avoid revisiting nodes. This pattern is the foundation for shortest path, level-order traversal, and flood fill.

exercise.prompt: Implement a BFS that returns nodes visited in order from a graph. Graph: 0->[1,2], 1->[3], 2->[3], 3->[]. Start from 0. Print the visit order joined by commas.

validation.message: BFS from 0 visits: 0, then neighbors 1,2, then 3
Use shift for FIFO queue and Set for visited tracking

exercise.hint: shift() dequeues the front node. Check visited.has(neighbor) before adding.
